text: The surgeon clamps the splenic artery at the initial stage of a spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy, focusing on the splenic artery first approach.
The main advantage is to decrease blood inflow to splenic vessels, allowing low bleeding during dissection.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery using fluorescence imaging, which highlights the vessel for precise visualization.
The surgeon clamps the splenic artery using a bulldog clamp, visibly reducing blood inflow to the splenic vessels.
The surgeon begins the dissection to separate the splenic vessels from the pancreas, carefully distinguishing these structures during the spleen-preserving distal pancreatectomy.
This is especially useful in case where there are strong adhesion of the pancreas with spleen vessels.
The surgeon performs a careful dissection near the spleen, navigating around multiple branches of the splenic vein and artery, ensuring minimal bleeding by maintaining control over the blood inflow.
The surgeon uses the splenic artery first approach to decrease inflow and control bleeding, ensuring that any bleeding from the splenic artery is managed effectively.
The surgeon removes the bulldog clamp from the splenic artery after controlling bleeding, allowing the artery to resume normal blood flow.
